data.title: Upstart Has Proven Itself - Current Premium Not Worth It
data.description:
UPST's Profitable/High-Growth Proposition Will Be A Lot More Compelling After A Deep Pullback

We previously covered Upstart Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UPST) in February 2025, discussing how it had demonstrated great resilience and growing relevance in the lending market, despite the challenging macroeconomic environment thus far.

Despite the promising performance metrics, the reduced loans held on the balance sheet, the growing funding liquidity, and the expanding profitability, it was also apparent that the stock had already pulled forward most of its upside potential, with its upward momentum seemingly buoyed by momentum, resulting in our reiterated Hold rating then.

UPST 1Y Stock Price

Since then, UPST has indeed pulled back deeply during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown, with the stock finally trading near our fair value estimates of $39.70, with the same sell-off also observed in its fintech peers in varying degrees.

Even so, thanks to the 90-day tariff pause, the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US and China, the still resilient US labor market, and the muted CPI numbers in May 2025, we can understand why market sentiments have already drastically lifted since the recent selloff.

This is significantly aided by the Fed potentially embarking on two rate cuts by the end of 2025, with it perhaps explaining why numerous fintech stocks have already rallied by the double digits since the recent bottom, UPST included, by the triple digits.

The rally has been further validated by UPST's raised FY2025 GAAP net income guidance from the prior of "at least breakeven" in the FQ4'24 earnings call to "be positive in the second half of the year and positive for the full calendar year," with it building upon the two consecutive quarters of positive adj EPS performance since FQ4'24.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the higher Transaction Volumes of 240.7K loans in FQ1'25 (-2% QoQ/ +101.6% YoY) and the total loan originations of $2.13B (+1.4% QoQ/ +88.4% YoY).

This is on top of the expanded loan conversion rate of 19.1% (-0.2 points QoQ/ +5.1 YoY), with the increased adoption directly triggering the growing revenues from fees of $185.47M (-6.9% QoQ/ +34.3% YoY).

These developments have validated UPST's AI-driven lending platform indeed, as observed in the growing funding availability from over 100 banks, credit unions, and private credit partners, which is also why the management has been able to move more loan funding away from its balance sheet.

This may be aided by the new agreements for its HELOC (home loans) products, as they "begun the process of moving funding off of our balance sheet" while potentially driving increased home loan originations ahead.

Combined with the highly successful cross-selling trends to its existing customers, we believe that the HELOC and auto loan offerings are likely to be UPST's growth drivers in the near term, as observed in their rich sequential growth by +51.8% QoQ/ +485.7% YoY and +41.8% QoQ/ +369.2% YoY, respectively, well balancing the stagnant personal loan growth.

As a result of these promising developments, it is unsurprising that the management has been able to achieve its bottom-line reversal faster than expected, thanks to the efficient consumer acquisition trends, the growing loan conversion rates, and the improved operational scale.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These have directly triggered the consensus raised forward estimates, with the UPST expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +32.3%/ +35.72% (based on the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00) through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +24.3%/ +5.7% (a year ago) and the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +31.1%, underscoring their robust execution despite the challenging past few years of macro/ geopolitical uncertainties and the next few years of normalization.

UPST Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded UPST's FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations to 37.60x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 45.52x, up from the 1Y mean of 103.51x/ 40.72x, the sector median of 10.88x/ 11.11x, and its fintech peers, including:

  • Block, Inc. (XYZ) at 11.91x/ 24.95x,
  • Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) at 322.20x/ 29.37x,
  • SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) at NA/ 63.23x,
  • LendingClub Corporation (LC) at 3.41x/ 19.19x,
  • PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) at 10.65x/ 14.79x, respectively,

demonstrating the former's rather premium valuations.

On the other hand, we are looking at a relatively compelling UPST's estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.27x, based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 45.52x, the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 (+3.4% QoQ/ +196.7% YoY), and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +35.72%).

This is compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.35x, LC at 0.36x, SOFI at 1.03x, PYPL at 1.67x, and XYZ at 5.07x, underscoring why UPST's profitable/ high-growth cadence may remain attractive upon a moderate pullback to our Buy zone, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

UPST's Overbought Levels

For now, UPST has already charted an impressive recovery by +110.8% since the recent April 2025 bottom, with the stock seemingly on its way to retesting the next resistance levels of $70s after running away from its 50/100/200-day moving averages.

Given the massive rally, it is unsurprising that the stock has also entered overbought levels by the time of writing, albeit with historical trends seemingly suggesting a durable upward momentum ahead.

On the other hand, readers must note that there may be increased potential for volatility, attributed to UPST's higher short interest volumes by +26.9% since the 2025 bottom and the rather exuberant stock market sentiments, with a pullback rather likely upon normalization in sentiments.

This is especially true since the Fed has turned hawkish in the last FOMC meeting, with it remaining to be seen how the tariff war may impact the US economy and the consumer spending trends in H2'25 once the 90-day pause ends by early July 2025.

This may be well-balanced by the One Big Beautiful Bill's supposed "ability to avert a sharp fiscal contraction in 2026," despite the risks arising from the drastically higher "federal deficit over the next decade."

For now, the intermediate-term uncertainties are why we do not believe in chasing UPST at current inflated levels, attributed to the higher dollar cost averages and the potential capital losses.

Based on the 1Y P/E mean of 40x (nearer the peer group mean of ~32x) and the annualized FQ1'25 adj EPS of $1.20 (+3.4% QoQ/ +196.7% YoY), it is apparent that the stock is trading at a notable premium to our updated fair value estimates of $48.00 as well.

While there remains an excellent upside potential of +63.5% to our bull-case long-term price target of $120, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $3.00, we believe that interested investors may be better off waiting for a moderate retracement for an improved margin of safety.

UPST 4Y Stock Price

Based on UPST's historical price cycle since the H1'23 bottom, we are likely to see the stock hit new heights before pulling back as traders take their gains, with a retracement to the December 2023 support levels of $47s likely in the intermediate term.

Given the potential -35.9% downside from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

UPST is a Hold here.

data.title: Village Super Market: Store Replacements Continue To Boost The Bottom Line
data.description:
Village Super Market (NASDAQ:VLGEA) continues to rally, and the reason for this may go beyond just the recent broad market rally among micro-cap and small-cap stocks. As discussed in my past coverage of VLGEA stock, this New Jersey-based operator of ShopRite-branded supermarkets has benefited greatly from closing down older locations, replacing them with new stores.

As seen in the company's latest quarterly earnings release, this strategy continues to pay off, given the impact of VLGEA's new store openings on revenue and earnings growth. Although this grocer's valuation is in line with its peers, especially when you take into account VLGEA's smaller size, status as a controlled company et cetera, there's likely still a path for shares to climb higher, due to both earnings growth and multiple expansion.

Furthermore, compared to other micro-cap/small-cap opportunities out there, VLGEA may not appear to be the most appealing, but perhaps is worthy of consideration as a defensive play, given the possibility of the current market melt-up giving way to a new round of volatility.

VLGEA Stock: Recap and Recent Quarterly Results

As discussed in my last write-up on Village Super Market, and as hinted-at above, the bull case here is pretty cut-and-dry. Over the last two fiscal years, VLGEA has experienced a steady increase in both revenue and earnings.

Again, a big factor behind this is the company's continued focus on replacing aged locations with new, fresh stores. Since early 2024, Village has opened two such locations. First, in March 2024, Village Super Market opened a replacement ShopRite store in Old Bridge, NJ. Second, last April, Village opened another ShopRite replacement location in Watchung, NJ.

Along with the replacement store strategy, Village Super Market has embraced digital ordering, another factor behind's improved fiscal performance. Before, there may have been concern that these two catalysts would have a sort of "one and done" effect on VLGEA's performance.

However, based on the company's latest quarterly results, it's clear they are still having an impact. On June 3, Village Super Market released results for its third fiscal quarter, ending April 26, 2025. Thanks to a 1.9% increase in same-store sales, coupled with the aforementioned new store openings, revenue increased 3.1% year-over-year, from $546.4 million to $563.7 million.

Thanks to the impact of increased sales, coupled with a slight decrease in operating costs, plus the benefit of increasing patronage dividends from Wakefern, the retail cooperative and ShopRite trademark owner that Village is a member of, earnings came in at 75 cents per share, up 25% from the 60 cents in EPS reported during Q3 FY2024.

For the nine-month period ending Apr 26, 2025, revenue was up by nearly 3.8%, with diluted earnings per share up by around 17% compared to the prior year's comparable nine-month period. Unfortunately, management doesn't provide much in the way of guidance in the company's quarterly earnings. Village Super Market also does not hold quarterly earnings calls.

So, is it uncertain as to what the future holds? Maybe, maybe not. It should be noted that the latest store opening happened just 17 days before the last day of the fiscal quarter. Hence, much of the new store sales growth was likely driven by the Old Bridge, NJ location opened last year, rather than the Watchung, NJ location opened this year.

In other words, the impact of this new location could be more pronounced in the quarters ahead, perhaps enabling Village Super Market to again report strong year-over-year results during the current fiscal quarter, which ends this month. If this occurs, VLGEA shares may be in for further price appreciation later in 2025.

VLGEA Valuation

At current prices, VLGEA trades for 10.3 times trailing twelve month (TTM) earnings, and at a TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of around 7.4x. Competitors to VLGEA, including Albertson's (ACI), Kroger (KR), and Weis Markets (WMK) trade at much higher earnings multiples. ACI trades for 13.4x TTM earnings, while KR and WMK sport TTM earnings multiple in the high-teens.

On an EV/EBITDA basis, however, VLGEA doesn't look particularly cheap. ACI, KR, and WMK all trade at EBITDA multiples of 7x-8x, in line with the valuation of Village Super Market. However, as noted before, Village Super Market's total enterprise value may be skewed, as outstanding debt and lease liabilities are countered by a hidden asset.

Yes, if you've read my past coverage of VLGEA stock, you'll know that in the past I've used an adjusted enterprise value for valuation purposes, by treating the company's outstanding loans to the Wakefern cooperative as equivalent to cash, and deducting them from the enterprise value calculation.

However, much like another "hidden asset" of Village's, the company's real estate portfolio, it's not as if the company is going to redeem all of these tomorrow, and use the cash to buy back shares or pay down debt. These loans are perhaps necessary, to sustain the operations of the retail cooperative.

Speaking of which, the fact that Super is a Wakefern/ShopRite member, this status is also a likely contributor to VLGEA's valuation discount on a P/E basis. The Wakefern cooperative, which provides product sourcing, advertising, and other essential services to Village Super Market, ending this partnership would likely cause major disruptions.

More importantly, this status as a ShopRite member likely precludes the company from ever being acquired by a major chain. In theory, another large Wakefern member could eventually acquire VLGEA, but there's only one member of this cooperative larger than that of Village Super Market.

Add in the fact that VLGEA is controlled via Class B voting shares held by the founding Sumas, and there's still much to suggest that a major re-rating is just around the corner.

That said, I reiterate my view that a further rise in price for this stock remains within the realm of possibility. Here's why.

Why Village Shares May Still be En Route to $50+ Per Share

Over the past year, VLGEA's earnings have increased by double-digits, with the replacement store opening in Old Bridge, New Jersey contributing to this growth. In the fiscal quarters ahead, the newly-opened Watchung, NJ location could have a similar impact.

Not only that, Village Super Market has another replacement ShopRite location pending, in East Orange, New Jersey. A newly-built ShopRite is slated to be the retail anchor at The Crossings at Brick Church Station, a mixed-use development in East Orange.

Unfortunately, there's not much publicly-available news out there about when this development is slated to open. The developer's website still touts an "expected delivery" this year. Still, whether the Brick Church Station location opens this fall, or maybe early next year, it would still likely have an impact on earnings.

These newly-built locations feature more modern features than the old-school ShopRites they are replacing, including a greater amount of floor space devoted to higher-margin premade and specialty items. As has occurred with the prior replacement stores, this points to further earnings growth.

Even if there are diminishing returns from replacement store openings, the opening of two new locations between now and the start of the next fiscal year could translate into another year of double-digit earnings growth.

TTM earnings right now stand at around $3.80 per share, add 10% to this, and we get annualized earnings of around $4.18 per share. At VLGEA's current earnings multiple, this suggests a valuation in the low-to-mid $40s.

Again though, do not discount the potential for additional, if only slight, multiple expansion. Since the growth resurgence first took shape, VLGEA has re-rated, from an earnings multiple in the high single-digits, to where it stands today.

In the past, the market has been willing to give this stock a valuation in the low-to-mid teens range. If re-rated to 12x on further earnings growth, VLGEA would be trading for over $50 per share.

Bottom Line on Village Super Market Stock

Beyond the latest store opening, and the upcoming store opening, it's possible that Village Super Market has another replacement store in the works. Per VLGEA's latest 10-Q filing, when discussing capital expenditures over the past nine months, Village Super Market references making a purchase of the real estate at its Springfield, NJ store:

While not for certain, this suggests that the company's Springfield, NJ location could eventually be redeveloped/rebuilt as a replacement location. A cursory Google Maps search of the store in question confirms that this is an older-style store ripe for a revamp.

If Village's ShopRite locations, which are primarily located in the New Jersey suburbs of New York City, continue to be replaced/modernized, the company may in turn continue to generate a steady increase in earnings. Far from the "value trap" VLGEA once was in the past, the stock may be well-positioned to continue performing well.

That said, as I've noted before, if the growth resurgence trend weakens, or if growth stagnates once again, the market may respond, perhaps even overreact, by sending shares back to their prior bargain-basement valuation.

Nevertheless, as the recession-resistant nature of grocery stores may help sustain the store revamp trend, irrespective of what happens to the economy in the coming years. Hence, there may be value in considering this stock as a defensive play among small-caps.

With this, consider VLGEA stock a buy at current prices.

data.title: Lessons Learned From My Development Days: 2 Shovel-Ready REIT Picks
data.description:
As many of my readers on Seeking Alpha know, I was a developer for over two decades, during which I built a variety of properties for clients such as Walmart, O’Reilly Auto Parts, Sherwin-Williams, Dollar General, PetSmart, and others.

I knew at an early age that I wanted to become a developer because it was in my DNA.

When I finished college, I decided to go to work for a small-town developer who had considerable land holdings in the county. I decided to work for him because I knew that I could gain valuable experience across property sectors such as shopping centers, office, net lease, and warehousing.

Unfortunately, towards the end of the business partnership, I gained insight into the riskier lodging sector, as my former partner decided to construct a hotel and conference center in our town. He was undercapitalized for the project, and that ultimately was the one torpedo that sank the ship (our partnership).

Until that point, my business partner and I co-developed dozens of successful properties, some making more money than others. Although I was not experienced in risk mitigation, I learned how to make each project more valuable by practicing trial and error.

For example, my very first lease contract was a gross contract in which the landlord was responsible for paying roof and parking lot maintenance, in addition to taxes and insurance (although there was a cap).

I remember getting a little snow removal bill, which was my responsibility, and this led me to always negotiate triple net leases where the tenant is responsible for all costs (taxes, insurance, and maintenance).

Another mistake I made early in my career was negotiating cost-plus contracts with general contractors. If you’re not familiar with this type of agreement, it simply means that the general contractor gets reimbursed for all expenses, plus an additional amount of profit.

In some cases (rare in my opinion), cost-plus contracts can help reduce costs, but in one of my larger projects, it led to an expensive $500,000 cost overrun that “your truly” had to eat. I now remind people that your contractor is only as good as your contract.

Over the years, I learned to add more contingency to every new deal, both soft costs (engineering, architectural, and finance costs) and hard costs (construction), and to always allow for weather delays.

I remember building a small shopping center in Boone, NC, for Advance Auto Parts and Sherwin-Williams. Before commencing construction, I was very excited about this project because I had two 15-year leases with high-quality tenants, and the initial going yield was around 11%.

However, I did not factor in the weather and the fact that the local municipality was going to be a pain in the butt. After closing on the land, it took months to get a permit to commence construction, and then I was further delayed with massive snow, which was especially challenging because the paving plans were closed for months and months.

I had a $2 million construction loan with interest ticking away with a burn rate of around $12k per month. It didn’t take long for the 11% development yield to become 9%. I was in Boone a few weeks ago, and I drove by to snap a photo of the project:

I sold the property to a friend of mine, and he has enjoyed the rent checks for over two decades. I regret not owning the property because it’s located on a high-barrier-to-entry site, but I remind myself frequently that I have 15,000 rent checks coming in every month with my stake in Realty Income (O).

There is little doubt that development can be risky, but for experienced investors, there can be a significant opportunity to capitalize on the wealth creation process “from the ground up.”

Currently, I’m working on ground-up projects in Costa Rica, South Carolina, and possibly Poland and the UK. Each of these is with experienced colleagues who have decades of experience within their respective markets.

However, for readers here on Seeking Alpha, I wanted to shine a light on two promising opportunities that have plenty of curb appeal. So, without further ado, let’s unroll the blueprint.

A Sunbelt Gem

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP) is an industrial REIT that owns 536 properties or around 63 million square feet of multi-tenant “flex” space across infill and supply-constrained submarkets. The company prefers to own last-mile e-commerce locations and shallow bay buildings (from 20K to 140K SF) as opposed to larger box buildings (140K SF and above).

So EGP’s customers rent space ranging from 20,000 SF to 100,000 SF, which makes the portfolio highly diversified – the top 10 customers represent just 7.1% of annualized base rent. 75% of EGP’s revenue is generated from tenants that lease under 100,000 SF.

In addition to its diversified tenant exposure, EGP also has an attractive geographic footprint with investments targeted in high-growth Sunbelt markets with an emphasis on local economies that are growing faster than the U.S. as a whole.

For example, EGP has around $100 million of assets in my hometown of Greenville/Spartanburg (6 buildings), including the buildings below at Access Point.

As of February 11th, EGP’s operating portfolio was 96.5% leased and 95.7% occupied by tenants with approximately 1,600 leases.

EGP has a development history, having constructed 269 of its own buildings since 1996 (representing 49% of the portfolio). These development properties total over 31 million SF with a total investment of $3.2 billion.

Although development is a riskier business model as opposed to acquiring stabilized assets, EGP has done a terrific job managing its balance sheet. As of Q1'25, the company’s debt to total market capitalization was just 13.7%, with debt to EBITDA of just 3.2x.

Take it from me, as a developer for over twenty years, having a fortress balance sheet can mitigate development risk considerably, and EGP has been a very conservative capital allocator. In fact, I would argue that EGP has one of the best-managed balance sheets in the entire REIT sector.

In January, the company refinanced a $100 million unsecured term loan, reducing the credit spread by 30bps, resulting in savings of approximately $1.5 million over the remaining 5 years of the term. In March, the company repaid a $50 million unsecured term loan and only has $95 million of debt maturing for the remainder of the year.

I always like to see how a company performs during adversity. As seen below, EGP did decrease adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, per share during the Global Financial Crisis (by 26% in 2010), it has since generated 10% CAGR over the last 15 years.

While I always like to see a company consistently boosting its dividend, EGP did not cut its dividend in 2009 or 2010, and as you can see below, the company has raised its dividend by 10% CAGR over the last 10 years. This demonstrates a steady hand within the EGP's management team.

In Q1'25, EGP generated FFO per share of $2.12 that exceeded the midpoint of guidance, an increase of 7.1%. In addition, the company provided guidance for Q2'25 of $2.13 to $2.21 per share and $8.81 to $9.01 for the year, and these midpoints represent increases of 5.9% and 7.2% compared to the prior year.

EGP shares are trading at 25.1x, which represents a satisfactory margin of safety compared with the company’s historical multiple of 30.3x. The dividend yield is 3.3% with a payout ratio (based on AFFO per share) of 80%.

Given the Sunbelt focus and muted construction starts (hit a post-pandemic low of 34.7 million SF) that lead to less first-generation space, EGP should continue to grow its revenues. Analysts are also bullish, forecasting AFFO per share growth of 9% in 2026 and 12% in 2027. I see a bright future for EGP, and based on the FAST Graphs chart below, I’m targeting annualized returns of 25%.

Not Your Ordinary Office REIT

COPT Defense Properties (CDP) is not your traditional office REIT.

The company owns 195 properties (22.4 million SF) that consist of 164 office properties and 31 single-tenant data center shells. The locations are very close to key U.S. Government defense installations and missions (CDP refers to as Defense/IT Portfolio).

CDP’s tenants include the U.S. Government and their defense contractors, who are primarily engaged in priority national security activities and who generally require mission-critical and high-security property enhancements. In addition, CDP owns approximately 1,020 acres of land that has a build-out potential of approximately 11.0 million square feet.

CDP’s defense tenant portfolio achieves steady growth from high occupancy and tenant retention, as well as lower CapEx. The USG represents 35.8% of the portfolio, and other top tenants include General Dynamics (4.8%), Northrop Grumman I2.2%), Boeing (2.1%), CACI International (2.1%), Peraton (2.0%), and Booz Allen (1.8%).

One of the biggest moats around CDP’s business model is its land holdings, mostly in knowledge-based defense installations in Maryland, Northern Virginia, DC, Huntsville, and San Antonio.

Since 2012, CDP has focused its capital allocation on its Defense/IT Portfolio that supports priority U.S. Defense Missions (90% of Annualized Rent).

CDP has an active development pipeline with $308 million of active projects (756,000 SF) that are currently 62% leased. The company has a ~1.2 million SF development leasing pipeline and ~1.5 million SF of potential opportunities.

CDP can fund $250–$275 million of development/acquisition investment on a leverage-neutral basis (cash flow after dividend) with no outside equity required.

Similar to EGP (referenced above), CDP also has a rock-solid balance sheet with a significant unencumbered pool of assets (97%). Secured debt accounts for only 3% of CDP’s debt outstanding.

Keep in mind that vacancy leasing is tied to DOD cyber activity, which has been steadily growing over the past 10 years due to growth in DOD cyber funding. Also, CDP's retention rate (~80%) is more than double that of the Office REITs (~40%) that report this metric.

With that in mind, CDP’s earnings history, dating back to 2007, has been lumpy. I haven’t been as bullish until recently, when the company began to recycle its office portfolio.

As shown below, CDP’s dividend history has not been as impressive; however, over the last three years, the company has increased its dividend by 10.9%. In addition, it has strengthened its balance sheet by lowering the average interest rate by 70 bps and reducing the % of secured debt from ~12% to ~3%.

In Q1'25, CDP generated FFO per share of $0.65, at the midpoint of guidance, a 4.8% year-over-year increase. Also, the same property's cash NOI increased 7.1% year-over-year. Also, CDP maintained 2025 FFO per share guidance of $2.66 at the midpoint.

As shown below, CDP shares trade at 14.6x P/AFFO, below the normal multiple of 16.5x. The 4.3% dividend is well-covered with a payout ratio of 63%.

I’m very bullish on CDP’s defense-focused business model and the appropriated budget increases and bipartisan support for future growth in Defense Budgets (expected to continue to drive demand for existing and new development).

The ~1.2 million SF development leasing pipeline and ~1.5 million SF of potential opportunities should generate enhanced peer-leading growth in future years. As shown below (using the FAST Graphs forecasting tool), I’m estimating annualized returns of 15%;

In Closing

“You don't learn to walk by following rules. You learn by doing, and by falling over.” Richard Branson.

My experience as a real estate developer has taught me more than the wisdom that I could have obtained from reading textbooks. Over the years, I’ve learned to become more risk-averse, always seeking to avoid the same mistakes.

I think this is the reason that I’m one of the most-followed writers here on Seeking Alpha because readers want to learn from a battle-tested stock analyst who has overcome dark days by one simple word:

Perseverance

As always, thank you for reading and commenting below.

Have a great 4th of July.

God Bless America.

data.title: Zillow: Continued Execution In A Tough Environment
data.description:
Zillow Group, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:Z) share price continues to hold up relatively well given the current state of the housing market and economy. This is largely due to the fact that Zillow is gaining market share and improving the monetization of its traffic.

I previously suggested that Zillow was swimming against the tide, with high interest rates and home prices depressing transaction volumes. This situation will not persist indefinitely, though. In addition, collapsing rents could be a boon for Zillow's rental business.

While the market landscape is in flux, and there is elevated downside risk in the near term, Zillow should continue to generate solid returns longer term as it captures more value from its dominant position in the industry.

Market Conditions

The current combination of high interest rates and home prices has caused the housing market to seize. Home sales remain extremely depressed, although there appears to be mounting pressure on sellers, creating a gradual shift towards a buyer’s market. For example, Redfin estimates that the US housing market has around 500,000 more sellers than buyers, with the imbalance particularly acute in the condo market. This is expected to pressure home prices going forward, especially if the labor market weakens. Current pressure in the housing market isn't readily apparent from most macro data. Rising rental vacancy rates and a sharp drop in rent for new tenants are perhaps most illustrative of the current situation.

Zillow's business is somewhat insulated from market fluctuations due to its relatively low transaction share and focus on realizing more value from each transaction. Despite this, if the market deteriorates enough, Zillow will be more seriously impacted.

Current housing market weakness is also changing the competitive landscape, with a number of companies trying to create both scale and an integrated portfolio of solutions. Zillow currently has the dominant online real estate portal, but market consolidation will likely increase competition between the remaining players.

The rise of private listings is a point of concern for Zillow, although this is a fairly minor threat at this point in time. Private listings are marketed outside the public Multiple Listing Service, meaning Zillow does not have access. In response, Zillow is banning listings from its platform if they have been publicly marketed elsewhere for over a day without MLS inclusion. While the company is trying to position this as an effort to provide transparency and access, it is a self-interested move that has created pushback.

Compass has filed a lawsuit alleging that Zillow is leveraging its market-leading position to try to stifle competition. This largely stems from Zillow’s decision to ban listings that have previously been listed privately. Compass has suggested that the policy restricts choice and that Zillow is attempting to dictate industry practice. Compass has also raised questions about Zillow’s tour request process, which redirects buyers away from the property’s listing agent to Zillow-affiliated buyer agents. It has been suggested Zillow collects up to 40% of the incremental buyer agent commission in exchange for this arrangement. The lawsuit highlights growing tension over data control, lead generation, and commission structures in an increasingly digitized real estate market.

Rocket Companies' recent acquisition of Redfin also threatens to reshape the competitive landscape. Similar to Zillow, Rocket is pursuing a “super app” strategy, with the Redfin acquisition providing greater vertical integration. This integrated model contrasts with Zillow's reliance on a network of third-party partners, though. The Redfin acquisition should lower Rocket's customer acquisition costs in its core mortgage business, which could pressure other players in the market.

The recent NAR settlement is another important development that could negatively impact Zillow, given its reliance on buyer agent advertising spend. The change has widely been expected to cause fee compression, leaving less money for Zillow to extract from the market. Zillow believes that it is well positioned though, due to the fact that its business skews towards higher-performing agents.

Zillow Business Updates

The Zillow story is largely about its Super App strategy at the moment. Zillow is introducing a broader portfolio of solutions which are integrated across the entire buying/renting process. This is helping to increase revenue per transaction, with the company having particular success in rentals and mortgages at the moment. Zillow's core business is supported by the fact that around two-thirds of home buyers are on its platform in any given month. Only a small fraction of that audience is converted into transactions, though.

Enhanced Markets are another important part of Zillow's business. In these markets, Zillow bundles real-time touring, home loans, and software-enabled premier agents to create a superior user experience. These solutions are being rolled out on a market-by-market basis, which is likely due to challenges related to training agents and scaling loan officer capacity. Zillow plans on making this experience available to approximately 35% of its customers by the end of this year and aims to eventually reach over 75% of its customers. 24% of overall connections in the first quarter occurred in Enhanced Markets.

Zillow Home Loans will likely be an important growth vector when the housing market inevitably recovers. Roughly 80% of buyers need a mortgage, making home loan origination and servicing enormous markets. Zillow has already achieved a double-digit adoption rate in its Enhanced Markets. Importantly, Zillow's platform should provide it with a significant customer acquisition cost advantage.

Showcase Listings are another important part of Zillow's business. This is a type of enhanced property listing that includes immersive 3D walkthroughs, interactive floor plans, and photo integration. These listings sell faster than similar non-Showcase listings, and for 2% more on average. Zillow is also making Showcase available to brokerages and individual agents, helping to extend its reach. Currently, Showcase is used for around 2% of new listings, against a 5-10% target. If Zillow can hit this target, it would provide an additional 150-300 million USD in revenue annually.

Zillow's Rentals business is trying to create a superior user experience by providing the most comprehensive supply of listings. Zillow has become the largest source of rental listings in the country, with over 2 million listings. Growth has been particularly strong in the multifamily segment, with 55,000 multifamily properties on Zillow Rentals at the end of Q1, up 38% YoY. Multifamily Rentals revenue growth was 47% in the quarter. Rentals growth is being driven primarily by an increase in the volume of advertisers and listings. This is being supported by the addition of listings from Redfin in March, which is reportedly performing better than expected. Growth accelerated in Q1 and is expected to continue improving throughout 2025. The company has set a long-term revenue target of 1 billion USD for this segment, against a 25 billion USD TAM.

Financial Analysis

Zillow generated 598 million USD in revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 13% YoY. While growth was solid across the business, Mortgages (~32% growth) and Rentals (~33% growth) were areas of particular strength.

Zillow expects 635-650 million USD in revenue in Q2, representing an 11-14% YoY increase. For Sale, revenue growth is expected to be in the mid-single digits, while mortgage revenue is expected to be up by around 30%. Full-year growth is expected to be in the low to mid-teens range, with approximately 40% Rentals revenue growth.

Longer term, Zillow believes it has a clear path to 5 billion USD in revenue, supported by increased conversions in the core business, a market recovery and rental growth.

Zillow's EBITDA was 153 million USD in the first quarter at a 26% margin, up from 125 million USD in the prior year's comparable quarter. Stock-based compensation expense was down over 10% YoY due to controlled headcount growth. Zillow still isn't profitable on a GAAP basis (excluding interest income). The company's margins continue to improve though, and the company should transition to profitability over the next few years.

EBITDA is expected to be between 140 million USD and 155 million USD in Q2 (22-24% margin), with the decline in margin driven by seasonal marketing and incremental lead costs from the Redfin Rentals partnership.

Zillow ended the first quarter with approximately 1.6 billion USD in cash and investments. The company repurchased 250 million USD of stock in the first quarter, and the board recently approved an additional 1 billion USD of repurchases.

Conclusion

While Zillow's share price has moved significantly higher over the past few years, its valuation is still reasonable. Market conditions present a near-term risk, but looking beyond this, Zillow is likely to continue generating double-digit growth over the long run. Margins should also continue moving higher, particularly once the demand environment strengthens. There are emerging threats, but I believe these are of secondary importance given how fragmented the real estate market remains.

Even if a downturn occurs, the impact on Zillow would likely be more modest than many expect. A return to a more balanced market, characterized by increased transaction volumes, would serve as a significant tailwind for Zillow, as it benefits more from transaction volumes than from price appreciation.

data.title: June Jobs Report Is Nail In The Coffin, No July Rate Cut
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported the monthly jobs report for June. The BLS reported that the labor market created 147,000 jobs in June, more than expected. The unemployment rate unexpectedly fell to 4.1%, putting it 0.2% lower than what economists expected, and even further behind the Fed's projections. The unexpected strengthening of the labor market has made the Federal Reserve’s job harder if it wants to lower rates later this month.

Leading Indicators

Going into the jobs report, leading indicators pointed to a mixed picture regarding the health of the labor market. Earlier in the week, the BLS released the Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for the month of May. The JOLTS report showed that the labor market was strengthening, with job openings growing to 7.77 million, the highest level of openings since November 2024.

When comparing job openings to the number of unemployed, the shortage of people in the labor market grew to over half a million, the highest level since January. When shifting to non-seasonally adjusted, the labor market is going in multiple different directions when it comes to the sectors that are hiring. Leisure/hospitality and information are the top two sectors with job openings growth, while construction, manufacturing, and government are seeing sizable declines in openings compared to a year ago.

From a flow perspective, hires fell in the month of May and remained under their pre-pandemic levels. While hiring is quiet, so are the separations from the job market, as they also continue to be below pre-pandemic levels. Even quits, which got a lot of attention during the pandemic, remain quiet. Essentially, the labor market has a broad lowering of flows, which is continuing to benefit its strength.

Leading into this morning’s jobs report, the weekly jobless claims data pointed to a markedly weaker labor market. Initial jobless claims, which are individuals seeking unemployment benefits, steadily rose from 235,000 to 245,000 on a weekly basis but did drop to 233,000 and were revised lower for last week. Continuing jobless claims, which incorporate all unemployed workers seeking benefits, saw a notable increase in June from under 1.9 million to over 1.95 million. Despite the growth in benefit requests, the unemployment rate fell.

Rather Broad Strength in the Labor Market

Several other indicators pointed to a continued strengthening of the labor market in June. The U6 rate, which includes marginally employed workers and those working part-time with the desire for full-time labor, is the broadest measure of unemployment, and it also fell 0.1% to 7.7% in June. While U6 has steadily climbed over the last two years, the measure has not weakened in four months, and the spread between U6 and U3 rates remains stable.

In addition to stable U6 readings, the number of full-time people in the labor market continues to grow. On a year-over-year basis, the number declined throughout 2024 but has been positive for the last seven consecutive months. This detail eases speculation that labor growth is coming from individuals taking on second jobs, versus unemployed people being able to find work.

Finally, wage growth, which represented a pain point during the labor shortage and inflationary era, has now become a strength. Average hourly earnings did rise by a less-than-expected 0.2% in the month of June, but that was in line with what investors should expect for a healthy labor market. On a year-over-year basis, average hourly earnings have increased by 3.7%, which continues to edge lower, but is strong enough not to raise concerns about workers’ wages.

Potential Structural Cracks within the Young and Old

While the labor market continues to be strong, it may not be working for everyone. Over the last several months, the unemployment rate among 20- to 24-year-olds has consistently risen and appears to be holding above 8%. In the two years before the pandemic, this level was consistently under 7%, hinting that there may be a structural employment issue in our economy as it pertains to age.

In addition to younger workers, older workers appear to be changing the labor market by leaving the workforce. The number of adults not in the labor force continues to rise and is now over 103 million for the first time ever. While many of these exits are retirements, the fact that the labor force participation rate among workers over 55 remains well below pre-pandemic levels should be noted. If these workers re-enter the labor market, there’s a good chance unemployment will rise, as it appears the supply and demand forces of the labor market are currently at an equilibrium.

Conclusion

While the Federal Reserve continues to insist that the labor market is going to weaken, and their leading data indicators seem to point to that, the labor market continues to strengthen and move away from the Federal Reserve’s 2025 and 2026 targets. The June jobs report, combined with persistent inflation concerns, should remove any doubt that the Federal Reserve will move on interest rates at its next meeting.

data.title: TUA: The Tao Of T-Bonds
data.description:
The treasury market has had a turbulent year so far, as investors continue to assess how America’s fiscal and monetary policies will shape the US economy in the coming years. For the Simplify Short Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TUA), these changes have helped boost its price; however, the current state of the bond market also raises some risks for this ETF that are worth considering.

This ETF maintains roughly 5X leverage in 2-year US Treasury futures contracts (while the rest of the fund’s assets are mostly short-term T-bills) to simulate the ICE 7- to 10-year US Treasury bond index. This fund is targeted toward investors who want exposure to the duration of 7-10 year T-bonds, similar to ETFs such as the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) – although the latter does so by holding US Treasuries at such maturities and doesn’t use leverage. Due to TUA’s difference in holdings, the fund has a higher duration of 9.67 years compared to the IEF’s seven. This difference plays a role in the reaction of each fund to changes in interest rates: The former has higher sensitivity to such changes, which could pay off in case of a rate cut. However, one factor that could affect the ETF’s performance is the shape of the yield curve.

The Yield Curve and TUA

Due to the high uncertainty in the financial markets regarding trade, fiscal policy, including Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill Act, and the Fed’s policy, the yield curve is significantly twisted.

Specifically, the yield curve appears to be inverted at the front end, so that the two and three-year bond yields are much lower than those of short-term bonds, while longer-term bonds are acting more “normal” as they rise along the curve.

What could explain this state of the yield curve? One factor is the term premium (TP) that provides compensation for the added risk of holding a long-term bond over a short one. According to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York calculations, the term-premium has declined for the 2-year and risen for the 7-10 year bonds; so that, the 7-year T-bond’s TP is around 35 bp while the 2-year’s is -10 bp (yes negative!). This sharp contrast suggests a high demand for the 2-year bond, as investors are flocking to such bonds, anticipating interest rates to come down. The market also signals concerns over the long term due to heightened uncertainty about inflation, likely stemming from Trump’s policies on trade.

Despite the decline in short-term rates, long-term rates haven’t fallen by much. This disconnect began in 2024, as shown in the chart below, when examining the 3-month, 2-year, and 10-year yields.

Looking forward, this disconnect could widen. Take, for example, the Big Beautiful Bill Act, which has recently been voted in the Senate and could become law by the weekend. It is expected to add $3.4 trillion to the US national debt over the next decade, according to the CBO. Such a rise in debt is likely to drive higher long-term T-bonds, even as short-term rates fall. The economic ramifications of this legislation may push up the term premium of LT T-bonds, making such bonds relatively cheaper compared to medium-term bonds, which are more affected by short-term economic outlook and inflation expectations over the next couple of years – these factors will play a significant role in the Fed’s policy.

Following the recent FOMC meeting, the Fed maintained its “wait and see” approach and still penciled in two more 25-bp rate cuts this year, likely in September and another in December; hence, the FOMC will likely start to signal a cut by the next meeting in July. However, they have also raised their projections for the rate outlook in 2026 and 2027, to include only one additional 25bp cut in each. In comparison, the markets are still expecting rates to come down to around 3% by the end of 2026.

These dovish expectations have pushed 2-year bond prices up and could affect TUA’s performance. While rising bond prices should help boost the ETF’s performance, if the prices of 2-year bonds rise significantly more relative to the prices of 7-10 years T-bonds, it could affect the ETF’s return as they roll over the future contracts quarterly. And since TUA has a higher duration relative to other ETFs, such as IEF, it’s more sensitive to interest rate changes, which could backfire if the markets are too dovish relative to the current Fed outlook, potentially leading to an upward course correction. One way a market correction could occur is through the progression of inflation.

Inflation and All That Jazz

While the term premium shows high uncertainty at the long end of the yield curve, inflation expectations have remained anchored for the most part.

So, for now, inflation hasn’t reared its head, based on the hard data, despite the high-end uncertainty over Trump’s trade war that could harm the progress the Fed has made in taming inflation. For the bond market, how inflation will develop could shape its progress, if the trade war, which has been marginalized in recent weeks in the news cycle, turn out to have only a transitory and limited effect on the economy and inflation, that would be good enough to let the Fed continue cutting rates, albeit at a more cautious way than it did in 2024. However, there is also a downside risk in which the Fed cuts more aggressively if the economic data shows signs of weakness – hence the high uncertainty in the bond market.

Bottom Line

The current state of the bond market is messy to say the least, while investing in bonds during times of uncertainty could be a prudent thing, this ETF might have some more complexity that could make investing in such an instrument a bit tricky considering its duration, the state of the current medium term bond market, and the yield curve so, while this investment may offer a higher return if and when the Fed were to start slashing rates again, as the market currently expects, the high uncertainty over how the economy will progress due to rapid changes in fiscal and trade policy could heighten the risk of this ETF not paying off.

data.title: Firmer U.S. Jobs Data Suggests No Fed Rate Cut Before September
data.description:
By James Knightley

Jobs report stronger than expected

The June US jobs report shows nonfarm payrolls rising 147,000 versus the 106,000 consensus with 16,000 of upward revisions to the past two months of data. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate surprisingly fell to 4.1% from 4.2% while the market was solidly backing the view that unemployment would actually tick higher to 4.3%. President Trump has been calling for the Federal Reserve to cut the policy rate 200-300bp immediately and two of his appointees (Chris Waller and Michelle Bowman) had suggested that they could vote for a rate cut as soon as this month's FOMC meeting. But the rest of the FOMC is far more cautious and today’s data indicates there will be no rate cut before the September FOMC meeting, especially with tariffs set to push inflation higher over the next few months.

Change in non-farm payrolls (000s)

Details are less rosy

Admittedly, the details are less positive with private payrolls rising only 74k versus expectations of a 100k gain. Government managed to post a 73k increase as a 47k increase in state government workers and a 33k increase in local government workers more than offset a 7k fall in Federal government. Leisure and hospitality added 20k and private education and healthcare services added 51k. So the story is that these three sectors - government, leisure & hospitality plus private education and healthcare services - have contributed 87% of all the jobs added in the past two-and-a-half years. The traditional sectors we would typically associate with a strong and vibrant economy are not adding jobs. We also note that wage growth was more subdued than expected, rising 0.2% month-on-month, while average hours worked per week fell to 34.2 from 34.3.

87% of all jobs are from just three sectors

Trouble may be coming

Our suspicion is that government, private education & healthcare services, and leisure and hospitality will become much less supportive through the year and potentially even act as a drag on job creation later this year and into 2026. Private healthcare jobs are vulnerable to the healthcare spending cuts that are part of President Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act and if consumers do start to become more cautious in their spending, reflecting the steep falls in sentiment, then discretionary spending on eating and drinking in bars and restaurants and other entertainment tends to be the first thing that gets cut. We also expect to see Federal government workers shrinking as a legacy of Elon Musk's efforts to get a grip on government spending.

Moreover, our sense that while hiring more broadly has been slowing as the economy loses some economic momentum, we are unlikely to see any meaningful job lay-offs is looking a little vulnerable to recent news flow. In particular the pick-up in Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification notices, which requires most medium- and larger-sized employers to provide notification 60 calendar days in advance of planned closings and mass layoffs of employees, suggests that layoffs could rise from late summer onwards. This tallies with the upward momentum in both initial and continuing jobless claims and the fact that the ISM employment numbers for both manufacturing and service sector firms have been in contraction territory in recent months.

Federal Reserve set to cut rates in the fourth quarter

In terms of what this means for the Federal Reserve, most FOMC members are cautious, wanting to see what the tariff impact on inflation will be before acting in response to potential labour market weakness. This was certainly the tone adopted by Fed Chair Jerome Powell at last week’s appearances before Congress where he stated, "we are well positioned to wait to learn more about the likely course of the economy before considering any adjustments to our policy stance."

With the uncertainty over inflation, the Fed would likely need to see clearer evidence of softness in the form of subdued payrolls growth and a rising unemployment rate to trigger an early move. Market pricing for the Fed this year has slipped from 67bp of cuts to 53bp in the wake of today’s report. We are sticking with our view of 50bp of rate cuts in the fourth quarter, with a further 75bp of cuts in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Globus Medical: Obtaining My Backing Again
data.description:
Shares of Globus Medical (NYSE:GMED) have taken a beating so far this year following a soft first quarter performance. Some of this did not come completely unexpected, as I urged for some words of caution in February after the company announced it would acquire Nevro.

This came after the company had seen a very strong 2024, including three consecutive guidance hikes, following the integration of NuVasive, which the company acquired before. The announced acquisition of loss-making Nevro earlier this year made investors uncomfortable (again), making me lose conviction in the thesis amidst demanding valuations.

Following a valuation reset post the first quarter earnings report, valuations have improved a great deal, with appeal readily seen at current levels, despite a hiccup in the first quarter and dilution overhang from Nevro.

Musculoskeletal Disorders

Globus Medical is a manufacturer of musculoskeletal implants, which are designed to treat patients suffering from spine and musculoskeletal disorders. The company offers a complete suite of innovative solutions for both open and minimally invasive surgery, as the company has launched some 150 products since its inception in 2003, on average launching 10 products per annum in more recent times.

The company has rapidly grown, both in an organic and inorganic manner, with the company competing against both larger and less-focused peers like Stryker (SYK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), to name a few.

Just half a billion business in 2015, the company broke the billion sales mark in 2022 and has grown to $2.5 billion here, largely driven by a $3.1 billion transformative deal to acquire NuVasive, essentially doubling the business with lower margin activities.

Sound operating margins around 30% a decade ago have come down to the high-teens in more recent times, largely driven by the acquisition of the lower margin activities of NuVasive. This deal and other growth have allowed sales to five-fold over the past decade, yet it has come with some 40% dilution incurred over the same period of time.

An $80 stock in February 2023 fell to $60 overnight as the company announced the purchase of NuVasive, as the market was skeptical. That move in isolation with the news made me upbeat in the aftermath of that deal, as Globus Medical was unleashed in 2024 after the NuVasive transaction closed in September 2023.

2024 – Delivering

Following the completion of the NuVasive purchase, the company saw real momentum in 2024. At the start of the year, the company guided for 2025 sales to grow by around 6% to $2.66-$2.69 billion, with adjusted earnings seen between $3.40 and $3.50 per share, driven by further synergies from the NuVasive deal combined with organic growth.

Trading around the $80 mark, the company was valued at $11 billion (on an enterprise basis), resulting in a 4.4 times sales multiple (based on the 2024 sales results).

Bigger news arrived in February as the company announced the acquisition of Nevro, causing shares to shed half a billion in value, after falling from $92 to $88 per share upon the announcement. That was a big deal as Nevro was valued at just $250 million, with Globus Medical set to acquire its struggling and much smaller competitor. The company generated $400 million in sales, yet these were loss-making sales, and hence, it would result in dilution to the 2025 earnings numbers.

The company upped the full year sales guidance to $2.80-$2.90 billion, which feels a bit soft with the deal contributing more than half a year in time to the results. Worse, adjusted earnings are seen down to $3.10-$3.40 per share, suggesting twenty cents per share dilution, equal to a $27 million reduction in dollar terms, although that accretion is seen in 2026 and 2027. This makes me hard-pressed to be upbeat, even as shares look cheap at just over 0.5 times sales (while Globus trades over 4 times sales), yet it comes with significant dilution to near-term earnings seen.

Trading around 25 times earnings, amidst a flattish net cash position, following strong share price action in 2024, I was becoming more cautious after decent returns seen already, and valuations getting more demanding.

Plunging

Since the cautious take in February, when shares were still trading in the eighties, shares have lost quite some ground, driven by a bad news report in May, which reset expectations with shares falling to the $55 mark at the time, now stabilizing around the $60 mark.

In February, the business reported 2024 sales at $2.52 billion, with spectacular sales growth reported at 60% thanks to the full-year contribution of NuVasive, with adjusted earnings reported up seventy-two cents to $3.04 per share. The company reaffirmed the full-year guidance for 2025, both in terms of sales and earnings, pro forma the Nevro deal.

Early in April, the company already announced the closing of the deal with Nevro, with first-quarter sales reported early in May. These were soft, down 1.4% to $598 million, with constant currency sales down 0.8%. This softness was attributed to softer closures, supply chain disruptions, and timing of orders, among others. Adjusted earnings of $0.68 per share were up five cents on the year before. While the company maintained the sales guidance, the adjusted earnings guidance was cut by another ten cents to $3.00-$3.30 per share. Net cash balances of $460 million, excluding lease liabilities, are sustained ahead of the $250 million deal for Nevro, which closed days after the quarter.

Pro forma net cash of more than $200 million is equal to about one and a half dollars per share, based on a share count of 140 million shares. The company is taking advantage of its financial position as the company announced a $500 million buyback program in May. Upon immediate execution, that results in a net debt load of $300 million, sufficient to buy back about 8-9 million shares at prevailing prices, equal to just around 6% of the share base.

What Now?

With 140 million shares trading at $60, the company commands a current $8.4 billion equity valuation and $8.2 billion enterprise valuation, pro forma the Nevro deal. With pro forma sales over $2.8 billion, sales multiples come in below 3 times, and even if adjusted earnings are seen down a bit further, they are still up compared to 2024. At current levels shares trade at about an 18 times adjusted earnings multiple, with this business having real potential to deliver on synergies, not just from the NuVasive deal, but certainly Nevro down the road as well.

Safe to say, that the organic growth is a bit softer, amidst the decline in the first quarter, although it is explained by some items. Following this 25-30% pullback from February levels, I am getting more upbeat again,

Following substantial share price declines, shares are back to levels seen in recent years, in fact, already seen for the first time back in 2018. Current multiples have come in a great deal, reflected both in lower sales multiples and earnings multiples, as the business operates with a rock-solid balance sheet, including net cash balances.

The pullback in the share price coincided with the purchase of Nevro and the soft first quarter, somewhat repeating the negative reaction in the share price of Globus when it announced the purchase of NuVasive. All this makes me more upbeat here, and while the first quarter undoubtedly was soft, it in part has to do with real incidentals, as it is too early to throw the baby out with the bathwater, but it feels too easy to blame softness solely on external factors as well.

Nonetheless, overall valuations look interesting enough to get involved here, as frankly, I am willing to get involved again with the shares, with fundamental support provided at current levels, despite a softer quarter.

data.title: Why Dell Is The Undervalued AI Giant You Can't Afford To Ignore
data.description:
In my last coverage of Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL), I rated the stock as a buy based on its strategic shift toward the Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) segment and the strengthening momentum of its Client Solutions Group (CSG) segment. Since then, the stock had a moderately positive performance rising 6.33%. However, investors remain cautious in light of recent announcements from top management.

The article below highlights the most important company news, as well as my expectations for the upcoming Q2 FY2026 earnings report.

Quick Update

Let’s begin with the most significant recent news for the company: Dell CEO, Michael Dell, has sold 10M shares and now holds 27.29M shares in the company. Investors may see this move as more cautious due to being an inside sell. While some investors may interpret this insider sale with caution, not necessarily reflect a negative indicator. In my view, this sell is probably a strategic reallocation of Michael Dell’s investment portfolio rather than a signal of declining confidence about the company’s future.

The good news includes Evercore’s bullish position on Dell, as the firm recently raised its price target to $150 from $140. Specifically, the brokerage firm estimates that Dell’s revenue from AI servers will exceed the already ambitious $15B projection. Also, Dell’s storage services are expected to further boost its overall growth due to the expansion of storage margin, according to analyst Michael Fisher.

Furthermore -and not so obvious-is that Dell becomes more attractive to investors through its projections to increase dividends, with the company projecting a 10% annual growth rate.

ISG and CSG Performance

As mentioned in my previous article on Dell, I believe the company’s strategic decision to shift its business model toward the ISG segment is a key driver for its performance.

As the table below indicates, the company has demonstrated consistent yoy revenue operating income growth from its ISG segment over the past five quarters. Additionally, in its Q1’26 presentation, Dell reported an AI-optimized servers backlog of about $14B and total orders of $12.1 billion and has orders of $12.1B – a YoY increase of 365% compared to $2.6B in f Q1’25.

Last about the ISG segment is that the company projected over 50% growth in AI-server shipments from FY25 to FY26, reinforcing Dell’s position as a key player in the AI infrastructure industry.

As for the CSG segment, I highlighted in my last coverage its strong positive momentum. In Q1’26, Dell reported 5% yoy revenue growth in this segment, but also reported a 16% decline in operating income, indicating possibly some margin pressure due to high competition.

However, the company is the leader in commercial AI PC and has increased its commercial PC market share by 6% over the past decade. This is a signal that Dell is well established to benefit from the upcoming end of support for Windows 10 on October 14, 2025, as mentioned in my last coverage.

Both the ISG and CSG segments demonstrate a strong track record, with even stronger potential for future growth. I still believe the company has significant room to grow and the full benefits of its strategic shift toward the ISG segment are not fully reflected in its currently reported results.

Valuation Metrics and My Expectations

Consider the nature of the industry it is better to focus on specific key valuation metrics and do a comparison with its main competitors. According to the company, its main competitors are Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), NetApp, Inc. (NTAP), Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI), Pure Storage, Inc., and Apple Inc. (AAPL).

Starting with trailing P/E and FW P/E ratios, Dell appears undervalued compared to its competitors -except for NTAP, as shown in the above table. Additionally, both fwd and ttm PEG ratios suggest that Dell is the most undervalued company among its peers, driven by the rising expectations for revenue growth. Also, the EV/EBITDA supports the undervaluation signals indicated by the previous metrics.

Before presenting my thesis, I would like to mention that analyst expects DELL to achieve higher growth than the sector median, as the below table indicates.

However, I don’t fully agree with the above fwd estimations. My main concern is that these estimations don’t reflect the company’s potential, particularly in the ISG segment. The company expects 8% revenue growth for FY26 and 16% growth for Q2’26. I believe these company estimates are reliable, and I give possibilities to Dell to exceed these projections.

The table above reflects my estimation for Q2’26. I project YoY revenue growth of 16%, which is in line with the company’s guidance and EPS to reach $1.64.

Risk Analysis

Geopolitical risks could have a significant impact on Dell’s revenue and growth because Dell operates in various regions. These risks include aspects that the company cannot always predict or control effectively. Although, the ‘tariff war’ between China and the U.S. seems to be coming to an end, still is a crucial aspect that could affect the company.

Also, Dell operates in a very competitive industry with many large players such as MSFT and AMZN. As a result, DELL may face pricing and margin pressure -as above-mentioned - which would lead to slower revenue growth and profitability.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Dell still is an undervalued stock with impressive revenue growth, mainly driven by its ISG segment. Also, its CSG segment leads the commercial AI PC market and the company is well-positioned to benefit from the cyclicality nature of this market.

Overall, its current share price looks undervalued. While analysts expect moderate revenue growth, in my view these estimates don’t fully reflect the company’s potential. Therefore, I remain bullish, and is a buy option for me.

data.title: NYLI Epoch U.S. Equity Yield Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equity markets were volatile again during the first quarter of the year, ending the period with mixed returns. Value indices were positive and outperformed growth indices which logged negative returns as the Magnificent 7* collectively fell into bear market territory. Most sectors were positive overall with energy, communication services, and health care generating the strongest returns. Information technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials were the sectors in negative territory.

After a strong start to the year with some indices hitting record highs in mid-late February, stocks ended the quarter on a weak note driven in large part by growth fears that followed uncertainty around tariffs and the broader Trump 2.0 policy agenda. Tariffs were one of the biggest stories in the quarter as ramped up tariff threats in March, declaration of "Liberation Day and an announcement of 25% tariffs on auto imports all weighed on investor sentiment. Softer data, worries over disinflation traction, and cracks in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth narrative were also among the catalysts that contributed to the selloff in the back half of the quarter. The quarter also featured some increasingly hawkish fedspeak although the Fed's latest Summary of Economic Projections continues to show expectations for two rate cuts this year. As expected, the Fed kept interest rates on hold at 4.25-4.50% during the quarter.

FUND INFORMATION (As of 03/31/25)

Portfolio Highlights

For the quarter, the NYLI Epoch U.S. Equity Yield Fund (the “Fund”) posted a return of 2.1% and slightly underperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index which returned 2.1%. The Fund captured significant upside when markets rose through the first half of the quarter and provided limited downside participation during the sharp decline in March.

Absolute return was positive in most sectors, with the largest contributions coming from health care and utilities. Health care return came primarily from strength in CVS and AbbVie. Both companies were boosted by strong earnings results and bullish guidance from management. Electric utilities contributed most notably to the return in utilities as the sector tends to be a safe haven in periods of volatility.

On a relative basis, the Fund slightly outperformed the broad market benchmark and finished behind the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index. Utilities were the largest contributor to relative return, owing to a mix of an overweight allocation to the sector and stock selection. Exposure to select electric utilities was a tailwind to performance. Conversely, the largest detraction came from information technology due to an overweight allocation and stock selection. Performance was hindered primarily by exposure to a few technology hardware stocks that were challenged during the quarter.

What Helped:

Among the largest individual contributors to return were CVS (CVS) and Philip Morris (PM). CVS is an integrated health care services company that operates a nation-wide chain of retail pharmacies, is the largest U.S. pharmacy benefit manager, and provides health insurance through its subsidiary Aetna. CVS's share price outperformed this quarter on the back of a strong earnings report where CVS posted strong results and a guidance that brackets consensus but looks achievable and a good set-up for beating and raising. On the earnings call, management was very bullish and highlighted making "material progress" in stabilizing Aetna's operation and bringing financial discipline to the business. The new CEO also stated that he is "very, very confident and very, very bullish on the continued recovery of that business." Management commentary provided investors with some relief and boosted optimism, sending the share price higher. CVS offers an attractive dividend yield that is well covered by free cash flow, and management remains committed to its capital allocation policy. Philip Morris is a U.S.-domiciled international tobacco company and the manufacturer of the largest international brand – Marlboro. Shares outperformed over the quarter which reflected a positive reaction to the company's fourth quarter results and commentary at subsequent industry conferences. More recently, investors have reacted favorably to weakness in the U.S. dollar (PM reports in USD but earns >85% of revenue in other currencies) as well as its profile as a safe haven in the face of macroeconomic concerns. Longer-term, Philip Morris consistently generates attractive levels of free cash flow and returns cash to shareholders through a significant and growing dividend.

What Hurt:

Among the largest detractors were Broadcom (AVGO) and NetApp (NTAP). Broadcom is a designer and manufacturer of digital and analog semiconductors focused on connectivity. It also develops and maintains software for mainframe applications. Shares underperformed as investors pulled back from AI themed names on worries of a capex slowdown. Early indications are spending on AI will continue to expand in 2026 despite DeepSeek claims of similar results at a lower cost. Revenues have yet to ramp-up and will need to for long term investment but near term the promise of AI is large, both in the transformation of industries and potential productivity gains. Being late or not committing to AI could have drastic consequence which should keep investment elevated over the near term. Broadcom returns cash to shareholders via an attractive dividend with a target of paying out 50% of free cash flow. The balance of cash generation is used to fund debt reduction, share repurchases, and/or accretive Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A). NetApp is a provider of network storage solutions for enterprises. This includes solutions for On-premises, Cloud, and Hybrid solutions. Shares were pressured by poor FY3Q25 results and lowered forward guidance. The company posted lower than expected close rates and pulled back expectations on U.S. government spending given current Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiatives. The close rate issue has been addressed with greater oversight. Government spending should resume at the federal level as agencies gain greater clarity on the new administrations' priorities as investment is needed to modernize systems. The company returns ~60-70% of its free cash flow back to shareholders, split between a dividend and share repurchases. The balance of cash generation is targeted toward tuck-in acquisitions.

*The Magnificent 7 stocks are a group of mega-cap stocks that drive the market’s performance due to their heavy weighting in major stock indexes such as the Standard & Poor’s 500 and the Nasdaq 100. It includes Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

Outlook and Positioning

  • While the U.S. economy has remained fundamentally strong, stickier than expected inflation alongside turbulence derived from rapid tariff announcements out of the White House have called into question soft-landing expectations. We expect some degree of inflationary pressures, demand erosion, impediments to economic growth and continued volatility as impacts resulting from the recently announced tariffs and a possible prolonged trade war. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs globally. The Fed is in easing mode, but the path forward for monetary policy has been clouded by growth pressures and upside risks to inflation stemming from tariffs. Going forward, supportive policy for the labor market will need to be carefully balanced against the risk of reigniting inflation. Markets' initial exuberance over Donald Trump's victory has reversed, as the aggressive implementation of broad- based tariffs has eroded risk appetites recently. The administration has chosen to lean on tariffs as bargaining chips in geopolitical negotiations, making it difficult to discern what policy proposals are genuine versus what is being used to posture for negotiating power. This uncertainty has driven skittishness in equities and clouded capital market forecasts.
  • Outside the U.S., the geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrop remains more challenging. A general uptick in geopolitical tension has remained an ongoing trend, with broad deglobalization and realignment of global supply chains via onshoring/reshoring and announced tariffs threatening to suppress long term growth potential.
  • We are cautiously optimistic on the path forward for the economy and for equities from a fundamental standpoint, but recent developments place more emphasis on "caution" than "optimism". With the way forward so clouded by the current trade climate, the wide array of possible outcomes is something that investors should be considering while managing risk exposures and positioning for the year ahead. We believe shareholder yield is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resiliency should growth begin to falter. The Fund offers a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and long-standing track records for sound capital allocation practices, providing durable stability and long-term consistency. History has shown that remaining disciplined has rewarded patient investors over time. No one can predict how or when the current tariff war will be resolved. But we do have a firm conviction that the types of companies the Fund invest in should have the ability to adapt and endure through this environment and even emerge stronger on the other side. At the portfolio level, diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against today's uncertainty and volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass.
